full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I shared with those of you who were here for our Thanksgiving service that I was a little bit giddy to be spending the holidays alone.<|10.28|> <|10.80|> My family was scattered with other plans, and I acted just disappointed enough to make them all feel just guilty enough to promise they would all be home for Christmas.<|28.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> laughter<|1.10|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It was a quiet day, and I loved it.<|6.00|> <|6.00|> It gave me time to start pulling things out for Christmas because my goodness, Advent was<|12.00|> <|12.00|> right on the heels of Thanksgiving this year, yes?<|15.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I can do everything at my own pace, lingering over the treasures of a lifetime, mine, my husband's, and those from our time together as a family of five.<|16.00|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And I'll be honest, I was also grateful for the quiet and the slow pace because while<|10.00|> <|10.00|> I was feeling gratitude this Thanksgiving, I was also feeling a heaviness in my soul.<|21.00|> <|21.00|> I have felt that for over a week now.<|26.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I've been thinking about today, about stepping into this pulpit again, again after events<|11.14|> <|11.14|> that unsettle us and break our hearts.<|15.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I stood here in this pulpit after Pittsburgh, after Christ Church, after January 6th, and after Evaldo.<|14.00|> <|14.00|> In its month of November, in a span of just nine days, Charlottesville, Colorado Springs, and Chesapeake, Virginia.<|28.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> My friends, I have no more words.<|5.00|> <|5.00|> Certainly not any new ones.<|8.00|> <|8.00|> "Lord, have mercy."<|12.00|> <|14.00|> And we know that we can't look at each other and say words like "shocked" anymore.<|21.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Or, we can do better.<|4.00|> <|4.00|> I can't say that anymore.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> We know what to do.<|11.00|> <|11.00|> As Richard often says, there are people of goodwill on both sides of the aisle who want to work together for policies that keep us all safer.<|25.00|> <|25.00|> We know what to do.<|28.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So then, how do we live through this?<|6.00|> <|6.00|> Through this particular season?<|10.00|> <|10.00|> How do we keep our internal landscape from crumbling when all around us things seem to be doing just right?<|21.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I think the season of Advent speaks to that very thing.<|11.00|> <|11.00|> The Pearsons have never been a family who wait until Christmas to put baby Jesus in the crush.<|22.00|> <|22.00|> Neither Don nor I grew up with that tradition, although I know Evans did.<|29.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But after placing my various nativity scenes out in the house this year, I went back and<|10.24|> <|10.24|> I collected all of those infant figures.<|15.24|> <|15.24|> I put them away, at least for now.<|19.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Because this year, I do not want to rush and skip over this small but visible reminder of the wade.<|11.50|> <|11.50|> This acknowledgement that we live in a time of darkness awaiting the light.<|17.50|> <|17.50|> We live in the meantime.<|21.50|> <|21.50|> A time that has been promised but is not yet.<|26.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We proclaim the mystery of faith in our Eucharistic liturgy:<|4.00|> <|4.00|> Christ has died, Christ is risen, Christ will come again.<|10.00|> <|10.00|> But not yet.<|14.00|> <|14.00|> Our collect this morning speaks to the mortal life between Jesus visiting us in great humility<|24.00|> <|24.00|> to his coming again in great majesty.<|28.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We live in the in-between time, almost not as it should be.<|9.46|> <|9.46|> Advent reminds us of that.<|15.56|> <|15.56|> Our reading from Isaiah speaks to this time.<|19.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Starting in days to come, the mountain of the Lord's house shall be established.<|7.70|> <|7.70|> All of the nations shall stream to it.<|12.60|> <|12.60|> People shall beat their swords into plowshares and their spears into pruning hooks.<|20.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Nations shall not lift up sword against nation,<|6.00|> <|6.00|> neither shall they learn war anymore."<|12.00|> <|12.00|> And while that beautiful spiritual revived during the Vietnam era by Pete Seeger,<|19.00|> <|19.00|> down by the riverside, has been running through my head all week.<|24.00|> <|24.00|> I see several of you nodding.<|26.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Y'all will be glad to know I will not be singing it for you.<|4.00|> <|4.00|> And you're welcome for that.<|7.00|> <|10.00|> It is helpful to have a bit of context here.<|14.00|> <|14.00|> In the first chapter of Isaiah, the people are lamenting that they have broken everything.<|23.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> All is lost. How we are feeling is not new.<|6.24|> <|6.24|> Chapter 2 tells them that it is God who can fix it.<|12.74|> <|12.74|> Come, let us walk in the light of the Lord.<|18.24|> <|18.24|> Light will months.<|12.00|> <|12.00|> And we enter this season of Advent with Matthew's particular bent on the life and teachings of Jesus.<|22.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> discernment, judgment, watchfulness, and the surprising presence of God.<|11.84|> <|11.84|> We can read this text from Matthew and have it engender in us a sense of dread and paranoia.<|20.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Or, we can look at it as a reminder that in this in-between time, God can surprise us.<|14.00|> <|14.00|> Watchfulness can also be a posture of hope.<|20.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We can find hope in the midst of the encroaching darkness in these shortening days that lead<|8.88|> <|8.88|> us to the first coming of Christ.<|14.88|> <|14.88|> My friends, I wonder, where are you finding hope right now?<|23.18|> <|23.18|> Where are you seeing the surprising presence of God?<|28.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I've been sitting with this question for over a week because I knew I was going to ask it.<|6.68|> <|6.68|> And I'll tell you where I'm seeing the hope.<|11.88|> <|11.88|> I see hope in those children that come and sit here on the carpet at this service every<|20.24|> <|20.24|> Sunday.<|21.24|> <|21.24|> I wish you all could have our view from behind the altar.<|26.24|> <|26.24|> It is priceless.<|29.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There is always at least one child who sits front and center all the way up to the edge,<|7.00|> <|7.00|> as close as they can get to the stairs.<|10.00|> <|10.00|> Their legs crisscross applesauce, leaning forward, watching as we celebrate the Eucharist.<|19.00|> <|19.00|> Watching for the surprising presence of God.<|25.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Last week, there was a child reverently posed in the most classic prayer position, head<|9.00|> <|9.00|> down, bowed, while reciting the Lord's Prayer.<|14.00|> <|14.00|> And there was another little boy on that side of the carpet who was doing what I thought<|20.00|> <|20.00|> were karate chops.<|23.00|> <|23.00|> But then I realized that I was going to be a little bit of a little bit of a little bit<|29.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> He was watching Dr. Boney in temp, and he was trying to imitate his conducting of the choir.<|11.00|> <|11.00|> And Michael, we would all agree that you are far more graceful and elegant than karate chops.<|19.00|> <|19.00|> But it was a most precious sight.<|25.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I see hope in God's presence in the 17 Eucharistic visitors who were new or renewing their certification<|10.00|> <|10.00|> last Sunday at a training so that they could carry the holy mysteries we share to those<|19.40|> <|19.40|> who cannot be with us in worship.<|23.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I see hope and God's presence in the fact that we who are many are one body, because we all share one bread, one cup.<|13.96|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> When I can look up from my screens and stop the doom scrolling, I can still see hope.<|11.88|> <|13.26|> And I can still see God's surprising presence all around me, especially in this place.<|21.10|> <|22.56|> Especially in you.<|23.96|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.22|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> All the rest I hold in prayer and I do what I can do.<|9.00|> <|12.00|> Maybe you needed this reminder today.<|16.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Most preachers will tell you that we preach the sermon we most need to hear.<|6.00|> <|6.00|> And I know that's what I did.<|9.00|> <|9.00|> I needed to hear this today, to remember.<|14.00|> <|14.00|> In these days of literal deepening darkness, in this in-between time, this mean time, while we wait,<|25.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We too walk in the light of the Lord.<|4.58|> <|6.10|> Amen.<|6.70|>
